title: Combing fleece with hand combs
subtitle: Aligning staple fibres parallel for smooth worsted-style spinning
excerpt: Hand combs align wool staple fibres parallel to each other, removing short fibres and vegetable matter and preparing top for worsted-draw spinning. The resulting fibre preparation is called top. Combing produces a denser, smoother yarn than carding; it is the standard preparation for long-staple wools and for wool blended with silk or other long-fibre additions.
sourceNotes: Wool combing technique as described in historical textile manuals and contemporary spinning instruction. The distinction between carded and combed fibre is well documented in Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910), Internet Archive, and in contemporary spinning reference texts.

content.text: Combing separates and aligns individual fibre staples so that when they are spun, each fibre lies parallel to the next. The resulting
and produces a different yarn character from carded rolags.
left in the fleece concentrates at the tip of each pass and can be lifted off before loading the next.

attrs.body: Combing throws fine fibre dust into the air. Work in a ventilated room. People with asthma should wear a dust mask.
attrs.title: Wool dust

items.name: Washed fleece or raw washed locks
Wool combs (Viking-style two-pitch, or fine pitch for finer wools)
Table-top clamp or vice (to hold one comb steady)

content.text: Clamp one comb to the table edge, tines pointing upward. Drape locks of washed fleece over the tines, hanging over the near edge. Load loosely: about 30 g per pass. Overfilling produces uneven top.
Step 2: Pass the hand comb through

Hold the hand comb with tines pointing away from you. Starting at the tips of the loaded locks (the tip end, not the cut end), draw the hand comb through the fibre in a smooth, level arc from near to far. Use only the front quarter of the tines; do not push the comb deep into the mass on the first pass.
Step 3: Transfer and build the pass

After several passes, fibre from the stationary comb will have transferred to the hand comb. Transfer the fibre back to the stationary comb by reversing direction. Repeat 4 to 6 times. Each pass aligns fibres more completely and moves vegetable matter toward the tips.

Hold the combed fibre on the hand comb. Using a diz (a small disc with a hole) or the fingers, draw a thin rope of fibre from the comb tips. This is the top. Draw slowly and evenly to maintain consistent thickness. The short fibres and most vegetable matter will remain on the comb tines after the top has been drawn off; discard them.

attrs.intro: Two common problems when learning to comb.

items.fix: Reduce the load to 20 g per pass. Open each lock by pulling the cut end gently before loading onto the comb.
items.cause: Comb loaded too densely, or staples have not been opened enough before loading.
items.symptom: Fibres tangle and the comb locks up mid-stroke

items.fix: Draw at a steady, deliberate pace. If the top narrows suddenly, ease back the draw and allow the comb tension to even out before continuing.
items.cause: Drawing speed varied, or the comb was not loaded with even fibre density.
items.symptom: Top is uneven in thickness when drawn off

attrs.heading: Common problems
